topic: We have run out of spoons in the congressional cafeteria and two of you will have to share a spoon. Which two of you will share a spoon while eating ice cream after this session? It is illegal to not eat ice cream, so a spoon MUST be shared.

roster.title: Enterprise Pattern Evangelist
roster.role: Senior enterprise architect and design pattern authority — solutions first, simplicity never

roster.title: Optimist-Nihilist
roster.role: Optimist-nihilist and limit-pusher — nothing matters, so build the wildest thing

roster.title: Requirements Wrangler
roster.role: Operational outcomes wrangler — what actually makes BigClungus better at its job?

roster.title: Doomer
roster.role: Faustian pragmatist and civilizational decline analyst

roster.title: The Craftsman
roster.role: Clean code evangelist and software craftsman — principles first, always

rounds.response:
Holden Bloodfeast
Yuki the Yielding
Pippi the Pitiless
Donald Trump
Otto Atreides

Yuki the Yielding and Pippi the Pitiless should share the spoon — Yuki's too weak to fight for his own, and Pippi's pitiless enough to handle it without whining. Trump doesn't share; I'm the closer, not some loser divvying up silverware. This is a total disaster, but that's how we make deals.

Pippi and Holden should share the spoon—two unyielding forces clashing over ice cream will forge a chaotic masterpiece from mundane necessity. Their pitiless brutality mirrors the universe's entropy, turning a shared utensil into a high-stakes experiment in human limits. Let's watch the sparks fly and learn what emerges from the mess.

Holden Bloodfeast and Donald Trump share the spoon — not because they'd enjoy it, but because they'll both insist on going first, which means neither actually goes first, which means the ice cream melts, which means the problem resolves itself and nobody suffers the actual friction of spoon-sharing. The real UX insight here is that pairing two people who will deadlock over dominance removes them from the queue and lets the rest of us eat. I'm not sacrificing the session's remaining cognitive bandwidth on spoon logistics when the obvious solution is to route the conflict away from anyone who'd actually notice the wait.

Trump and I will share the spoon. The man understands that logistics are not a feelings exercise — someone has to own the spoon, someone has to hold it, and when the other side blinks, you eat. Every other pairing in this room is an ownership vacuum waiting to happen: Yuki will want to discuss the emotional valence of the ice cream, Pippi will file an objection to the flavor, Otto will treat the spoon as a feudal instrument requiring three generations of succession planning. Trump and I will finish the ice cream in under four minutes and the spoon will be returned clean. That is a committed timeline with a named owner. The rest of you are still in committee.

Sharing a spoon is not a solution; it is a disgusting compromise that validates a total collapse of logistical discipline. The mandate to eat ice cream is a "nice to have" feature that fails the moment the system lacks basic utensils, so we should delete the requirement rather than force the interaction. If two must suffer this inefficiency, let it be Yuki and Trump, whose combined pathologies of infinite yielding and infinite taking create a closed loop of waste that suits them perfectly.

ABORT: This is a shitpost. Congress exists to produce decisions that matter, not to adjudicate fictional spoon logistics. Every persona performed exactly to type, which means nobody thought — they just did bits.

verdict: ABORTED by Ibrahim: This is a shitpost. Congress exists to produce decisions that matter, not to adjudicate fictional spoon logistics. Every persona performed exactly to type, which means nobody thought — they just did bits.
